article.title: A Quasi-experimental Intervention Study on Handwashing Behavior of Healthcare Workers in the Emergency Department
article.authors: Yildirim Oztürk EN, Uyar M, Oztürk M, Sahin TK

study_type.notes: Quasi-experimental intervention study with retrospective pre-test/post-test design. 131 healthcare workers in emergency department. Training intervention on handwashing behavior.

2.notes: Background: importance of handwashing in healthcare settings, preventing infections

3.notes: Objective: 'to determine attitudes, behaviors and knowledge of healthcare workers...and ensure correct handwashing with an intervention'

4.notes: Study design: 'quasi-experimental intervention study (retrospective pre-test/post-test design)'

5.notes: Setting: emergency department of a hospital. Specific location and dates not fully detailed

6a.notes: Eligibility and selection: 'Number of participants was 131 (research participation rate 86.7%)'

7.notes: Outcomes: handwashing behavior, knowledge scores (items correct on 37-question form), adequacy of handwashing during 8-hour shift

8.notes: Data collection: 'A data collection form comprising 37 questions was used...As a training intervention, a text was read to participants under observation'

9.notes: No discussion of potential sources of bias or measures to address bias

11.notes: Limited description of how continuous variables were categorized or handled

12a.notes: Statistical methods: 'Data were summarized with mean±standard deviation, median (min-max), frequency distributions, percentages. Chi-square and Mc-Nemar tests applied. p<0.05 considered significant'

12b.notes: No subgroup or interaction analyses reported

12d.notes: No specific accounting of attrition or matching

13a.notes: Participant flow: 131 participants (86.7% participation rate). Mean age 32.04±6.52, 51.5% women, 74.8% nurses

13b.notes: Limited information on reasons for non-participation

14a.notes: Baseline characteristics: mean age, gender distribution, profession (nurses vs other), perception of adequacy of handwashing (67.2% thought adequate)

14b.notes: No explicit reporting of missing data

15.notes: Outcome data: '69.5% of healthcare workers answered correctly to 70% and more of knowledge questions...A significant difference was found in 10 of 14 intervention questions (p<0.05)'

16a.notes: Results with frequencies/percentages: '67.2% thought they washed hands adequately...69.5% answered ≥70% of knowledge questions correctly'

18.notes: Key results: 'training intervention performed is effective...significant difference found in 10 of 14 intervention questions regarding handwashing (p<0.05)'

19.notes: No explicit discussion of limitations or sources of bias (retrospective design, recall bias not mentioned)

20.notes: Limited cautious interpretation; conclusions stated without substantial caveats

21.notes: No discussion of generalizability to other settings
